cards.text: The {{c1::this}} pointer is implicitly passed to all non-static member functions and points to the {{c2::object the function was called on}}.
Destructors are called automatically when an object {{c1::goes out of scope}} and are used to {{c2::clean up resources}} like dynamically allocated memory.
Static member variables are {{c1::shared by all objects}} of the class and must be {{c2::defined outside the class}} definition.
Friend functions and friend classes can access {{c1::private and protected}} members, but friendship is {{c2::not reciprocal}} or {{c3::transitive}}.

cards.front: What are the three types of members a class can have in terms of scope?
cards.back:
• Non-static members: belong to each object
  • Static members: shared by all objects
  • Friend functions: not members, but have access to private data

cards.front: Why must class template member functions typically be defined in header files?
cards.back: Because the compiler needs the full definition to instantiate the template with specific types. Separating into .cpp files would cause linker errors.

cards.question: What's wrong with this class design?
cards.answer: Memory leak - the class allocates memory but never deletes it.
cards.explanation: Need to add destructor: ~Resource() { delete[] m_data; } to implement RAII properly.

cards.question: Which members can this static member function access?
cards.answer: Only s_count (static member). Cannot access m_value (non-static member).
cards.explanation: Static member functions have no this pointer, so they can only access static members.

cards.concept: Key principles of class organization in Chapter 15
cards.explanation:
Best practices covered in Chapter 15:
  • Separate class definitions (.h) from implementations (.cpp)
  • Use RAII for automatic resource management
  • Use static members for class-wide data and behavior
  • Use friend functions sparingly (they break encapsulation)
  • Put template code in headers
  • Use nested types to organize related types

cards.back: Resource Acquisition Is Initialization - an idiom where resources are acquired in constructors and released in destructors
